title: Edition numbering and the colophon page
subtitle: The conventions for numbering a limited print run and writing a zine colophon
excerpt: A hand-numbered limited edition is one of the few things a home-printed zine can do that a commercially printed magazine cannot. Edition numbering, a colophon page, and a hand-signed title page together give a small-run zine the status of a produced object rather than a photocopy. This tutorial covers the conventions and the practical decisions: how many copies to number, what goes in a colophon, and where they appear.
sourceNotes: Edition numbering and colophon conventions derive from the fine printing and artists' book tradition of the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. The colophon as a publishing convention is documented in printing manuals and bibliographic references from the nineteenth century onward.

glossaryTerms.definition: A page at the end of a book or zine giving production details: title, author, date, edition size, printing method, and any credits. From the Greek 'finishing touch'. In artists' books and zines, it is also the place for the handwritten edition number and signature.

The fraction handwritten on each copy of a limited print run, 1/50, 2/50, indicating which copy this is (numerator) out of the total edition size (denominator). A convention from print and artists' book publishing.

Copies printed outside the numbered edition, author copies, review copies, or archival copies. Written as 'A/P' (Artist's Proof) or 'H.S.' (Hors Série) rather than numbered. Standard in edition printing; optional in zines.

content.text: is a practical page with a clear purpose: it tells the reader who made the thing, when, how, and in what quantity. For a small zine it is also a declaration of intent, this is a finished publication, not a draft.
A minimal colophon needs four things: title, author name or alias, date, and edition size. Optional: printing method, paper stock, credits, and a contact address.
Decide the total edition size before printing. Print exactly that number plus a few extras for your own archive (these become the

Number each copy by hand in pencil or pen: write the fraction (for example, 7/30) on the colophon page or the title page. The hand-writing is the point, a rubber-stamped number is acceptable; a printed one defeats the purpose.
Sign below the edition number. Your name, initials, or alias, whatever you use as the author identification for this zine.
Keep a record of who received which number if you are distributing copies, it is useful for reprints and for tracking your own distribution. A simple list in a notebook is sufficient.
Placing the colophon in the layout

In book publishing tradition the colophon goes on the last page (verso) or the back of the title page. In a saddle-stitched zine, the final recto (page 7 in an 8-page zine) or the inner back cover are the natural positions. The colophon text can be set in a smaller type size or a different style from the main text, it is production information, not editorial content.

items.symptom: The edition size stated in the colophon is wrong, more or fewer copies were printed
items.cause: The colophon was written before the final print run count was confirmed.
items.fix: Print the colophon page last, or leave a blank space for the edition number and fill it in by hand after the run. Never state an edition size you have not yet confirmed.

items.symptom: Numbers are inconsistent across the run, some smeared, some too faint
items.cause: Numbering was rushed or done with an inconsistent pen pressure.
items.fix: Work through the entire edition in one sitting with the same pen. Test the pen on scrap paper first.
